title: Client Instance Assertion — User Delegation (authorization_code)
description: A user authorizes an AI agent via OAuth authorization code. When the agent redeems the code, it presents its Client Instance Assertion as the actor_token. The resulting access token carries user delegation: sub=user, act.sub=agent instance. This is the 'delegation' case.

steps.label: GET /authorize (User Consent)
steps.response_body: <html>Consent page: Allow 'Codex Agent' to access your repos?</html>
steps.explanation: The user visits the authorization endpoint (redirected from the agent's frontend). The AS authenticates the user, displays a consent screen, and the user grants the agent access to their repositories. PKCE code_challenge is included for security.
annotations: This is a standard OAuth 2.1 authorization_code flow. The instance assertion is NOT involved at this stage.
PKCE (code_challenge) protects against authorization code interception attacks.
The client_id here is the logical OAuth client (the agent service), not any specific instance.
§8.4: The instance assertion presented at step 5 MUST go to the same AS that issued this authorization code.

steps.label: 302 Redirect + Auth Code
steps.explanation: The AS redirects the user's browser back to the agent's redirect_uri with a single-use authorization code. The code is bound to the PKCE code_challenge and the authenticated user. The agent receives the code when the browser follows the redirect.
annotations: The authorization code is single-use and short-lived (typically 60–600 seconds).
state parameter echoed back prevents CSRF attacks.
The code is NOT an instance assertion — it only identifies the authorization grant (user + client + scope).
The agent instance identity is established separately in step 5, at token redemption time.

steps.label: Request Instance Assertion
steps.explanation: With the authorization code in hand, the agent instance requests its Client Instance Assertion from the Instance Issuer before redeeming the code. The issuer authenticates the runtime via platform attestation and verifies it is authorized to act as this client_id.
annotations: §7.1: Issuer MUST authenticate the runtime. Common: Kubernetes SPIFFE workload identity via SPIRE agent.
The assertion must name this same AS as audience — assertion cannot be reused at a different AS.
The agent may pre-fetch the assertion before receiving the code, but it must not be expired by token redemption time.
§7.2: Recommended exp window ≤5 minutes — short enough to bound the trust-withdrawal latency.

steps.label: Instance Assertion JWT
1. JOSE Header
steps.value:
{
  "alg": "ES256",
  "kid": "4vC8agycHu6rnkEEJYAH6VuCe4JoSkPV",
  "typ": "client-instance+jwt"
}

§7.3: typ MUST be 'client-instance+jwt'.
Prevents type-confusion with:
  • WIMSE workload credentials
  • Raw SPIFFE JWT-SVIDs (except §8.8.1 SPIFFE compat mode)
  • OAuth access tokens (at+jwt)
  • OAuth client assertions (JWT)

steps.label: 2. JWT Payload
steps.value:
{
  "iss": "https://workload.openai.example.com",
  "sub": "spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/session-xyz",
  "aud": "https://as.example.com",
  "client_id": "https://openai.example.com/codex",
  "iat": 1770001000,
  "nbf": 1770001000,
  "exp": 1770001300,
  "jti": "unique-id-1",
  "cnf": { "jkt": "0ZcOCORZNYyOWyYiguA4I" },
  "sub_profile": "client_instance"
}

NOTE: Different session-xyz instance from self-acting scenario.
Each container/pod/function gets a unique sub identifier.
Instance identifiers MUST NOT be reused (§12.2).

cnf.jkt = JWK SHA-256 thumbprint (RFC 9449 §3.1) of the instance key.
MUST NOT contain an act claim (§7.2).

steps.label: 3. Client Metadata (Trust Anchor)
steps.value:
The AS looks up client metadata for:
https://openai.example.com/codex

Relevant section:
{
  "client_id": "https://openai.example.com/codex",
  "token_endpoint_auth_method": "private_key_jwt",
  "jwks_uri": "https://openai.example.com/codex/jwks.json",
  "instance_issuers": [{
    "issuer": "https://workload.openai.example.com",
    "jwks_uri": "https://workload.openai.example.com/jwks.json",
    "subject_syntax": "spiffe",
    "trust_domain": "openai.example.com",
    "spiffe_id": "spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/*",
    "signing_alg_values_supported": ["ES256"]
  }]
}

§6.1.1: By listing this issuer in instance_issuers, the client
delegates instance attestation authority to the issuer.
This metadata IS the trust anchor — the AS trusts it as authoritative.
§6.1.1: If instance_issuers is absent or empty, the AS MUST NOT
accept client-instance-jwt assertions for this client.

steps.explanation: The Instance Issuer returns the signed Client Instance Assertion. The instance (session-xyz) is a different container/pod from session-abc in the self-acting scenario — each runtime gets its own unique SPIFFE ID. The cnf.jkt seeds the key-binding that the AS will enforce when issuing the final access token.
annotations: §12.2: Instance identifiers MUST be unique and MUST NOT be reused for different runtimes.
§7.3: typ = 'client-instance+jwt' is mandatory — AS rejects any other value.
§7.2: exp is set to 1770001300 — exactly 300 seconds (5 min) from iat. Satisfies §7.2 maximum.
§7.2: sub_profile = 'client_instance' — RECOMMENDED. Lets RS apply instance-class-aware policy.

steps.label: POST /token (authz_code)

artifacts.signing_key_hint: Two JWTs presented: client_assertion (client's key) + actor_token (instance issuer's key)
artifacts.final_output: Two independent JWT signatures in one token request

steps.label: 1. client_assertion (Client Auth)
steps.value:
client_assertion_type:
  urn:ietf:params:oauth:client-assertion-type:jwt-bearer

client_assertion JWT (private_key_jwt):
{
  "iss": "https://openai.example.com/codex",
  "sub": "https://openai.example.com/codex",
  "aud": "https://as.example.com/token",
  "exp": 1770001360,
  "iat": 1770001020,
  "jti": "client-assertion-jti-001"
}
Signed with: client's private key (from jwks_uri)

This authenticates the LOGICAL CLIENT (the agent service).
It is SEPARATE from the instance assertion.

Required because token_endpoint_auth_method = 'private_key_jwt'.
If method were 'client_instance_jwt', only actor_token would be needed
(the actor_token doubles as client authentication in that case).

steps.label: 2. actor_token (Instance Assertion)
steps.value:
actor_token = Client Instance Assertion from step 4
actor_token_type =
  urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:client-instance-jwt

This identifies the SPECIFIC RUNTIME INSTANCE.
It is a separate JWT, signed by the Instance Issuer.

DISTINCTION:
• client_assertion → authenticates the OAuth client identity
• actor_token → authenticates the specific container/process

Both are required when token_endpoint_auth_method=private_key_jwt.
If method were client_instance_jwt, only actor_token is needed.

steps.label: 3. Full Token Request
steps.value:
grant_type=authorization_code
&code=SplxlOBeZQQYbYS6WxSbIA
&redirect_uri=https://agent.example.com/callback
&code_verifier=dBjftJeZ4CVP-mB92K27uhbUJU1p1r_wW1gFWFOEjXk
&client_id=https://openai.example.com/codex
&client_assertion_type=
  urn:ietf:params:oauth:client-assertion-type:jwt-bearer
&client_assertion=<client private_key_jwt>
&actor_token=<Client Instance Assertion JWT from step 4>
&actor_token_type=
  urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:client-instance-jwt

DPoP: <proof in header — same key as cnf.jkt in assertion>

§4.1: authorization_code is a permitted grant type for
actor_token (this spec extends beyond RFC 8693 token-exchange).

§8.4: AS MUST verify client_id at /token matches the client_id
that received the authorization code (authorization-time consistency).
§8.4: AS MUST NOT permit instance identity to bypass standard
authorization-code controls (single-use, redirect URI, code challenge).

steps.explanation: The agent instance redeems the authorization code. This request carries both a client_assertion (authenticating the logical client via private_key_jwt) AND an actor_token (the Client Instance Assertion identifying this specific runtime). Together they answer: which client? (client_assertion) and which instance of that client? (actor_token). The DPoP proof binds everything to the instance's private key.
annotations: §4.1: This spec extends actor_token to authorization_code grant — not permitted in RFC 8693 (token exchange only).
Two-level authentication: client_assertion = logical client identity; actor_token = specific runtime instance.
§8.4: The instance assertion MUST be presented to the same AS that issued the authorization code.
§8.2 Step 9: AS checks authorization-time consistency — the authorization code was bound to this client_id.

steps.label: 200 OK + Access Token

artifacts.final_output: AS-signed JWT Access Token with Delegation Structure

steps.label: 1. AS Validation Steps (§8.2)
steps.value:
Processing for authorization_code with private_key_jwt auth:
1. Validate authorization code (user='user@example.com' authorized)
2. Verify client_assertion (private_key_jwt) → client authenticated
3. actor_token_type = client-instance-jwt → instance assertion path
4. Resolve client metadata for https://openai.example.com/codex
5. Parse actor_token, read iss='https://workload.openai.example.com'
6. Find descriptor in instance_issuers where issuer matches iss
7. Fetch JWKS from descriptor's jwks_uri
8. Verify ES256 signature on instance assertion
9. Validate typ = 'client-instance+jwt' (reject any other value)
10. Validate: aud = this AS, exp not expired, jti not replayed
11. Validate: client_id in assertion = authenticated client_id ✓
12. §8.4: Verify assertion's AS = authorization code's AS ✓
13. Verify DPoP proof; JWK thumbprint = cnf.jkt ✓
14. §8.6.1: grant_type=authorization_code → DELEGATION case
15. Populate act claim per §8.6.2

steps.label: 2. Access Token (Delegation Case, §8.6.2)
steps.value:
{
  "iss": "https://as.example.com",
  "aud": "https://api.example.com",
  "sub": "user@example.com",
  "client_id": "https://openai.example.com/codex",
  "scope": "openid repo.write",
  "cnf": { "jkt": "0ZcOCORZNYyOWyYiguA4I" },
  "iat": 1770001030,
  "exp": 1770004630,
  "act": {
    "sub": "spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/session-xyz",
    "iss": "https://workload.openai.example.com",
    "cnf": { "jkt": "0ZcOCORZNYyOWyYiguA4I" },
    "sub_profile": "client_instance"
  }
}

DELEGATION CASE (§8.6.2):
• sub = the user who authorized (USER is principal)
• act.sub = actor_token's sub (INSTANCE is actor)
• act.iss = actor_token's iss (for auditability)
• act.cnf = actor_token's cnf (instance key for RS sender-constraint)
• act.sub_profile = actor_token's sub_profile — SHOULD include
• Top-level cnf = same instance key (bound via DPoP at token endpoint)

§8.6.1: Grant type determines case:
  authorization_code → delegation (act claim present)
  client_credentials → self-acting (no act claim)

steps.label: 3. Compare: Delegation vs Self-Acting

steps.explanation: The AS issues an access token with delegation structure. The user remains the principal (sub), while the specific agent instance appears in the act claim. Both the top-level cnf and act.cnf carry the instance key's thumbprint. Resource servers can now enforce policy based on both who (the user) and which instance (the agent) made the request.
annotations: §8.6.2: DELEGATION case — sub = user principal, act.sub = instance actor. Contrast with self-acting where sub = instance.
§8.6.1: Grant type determines the case: authorization_code → delegation. client_credentials → self-acting.
The act.cnf lets the RS verify sender-constraint specifically for the acting instance, not just the top-level cnf.
§8.6.4: If there were a prior act chain (e.g., agent spawning a sub-agent), the new instance becomes the outermost act and prior chain nests as act.act.

steps.label: GET /api/repos (Delegated)

artifacts.raw_input: DPoP proof for delegated resource access
artifacts.signing_key_hint: Instance ES256 Private Key — same key as act.cnf.jkt
artifacts.final_output: DPoP Proof Signature for Delegated Resource Request

steps.label: 1. RS Policy on Delegated Token (§9)
steps.value:
The Resource Server sees this access token structure:
{
  "sub": "user@example.com",          ← the USER authorized this
  "act": {
    "sub": "spiffe://...session-xyz", ← this INSTANCE is presenting it
    "sub_profile": "client_instance"
  },
  "scope": "repo.write",
  "cnf": { "jkt": "..." }             ← instance's key
}

RS can now make DUAL policy decisions:
1. User-based: Does user@example.com have access to this repo?
2. Instance-based: Is this a known instance type ('client_instance')?
   Should this instance be allowed to act on the user's behalf?
3. Scope-based: Is repo.write authorized for this request?

§9: RS MUST NOT rely on act.iss for proof of possession.
Top-level cnf is the authoritative key binding (not act.cnf).

steps.label: 2. Audit Trail
steps.value:
The delegation structure enables forensic-quality audit logging:

Who authorized: user@example.com
Which client: https://openai.example.com/codex
Which instance: spiffe://openai.example.com/codex/session-xyz
Instance issuer: https://workload.openai.example.com
Instance key: JWK thumbprint 0ZcOCORZNYy...iguA4I
Requested scope: openid repo.write
Action: GET /api/repos

This audit record answers:
  'Which specific container of the Codex agent, running
  at what time, accessed user@example.com's repos?'

steps.explanation: The agent instance calls the resource server with the delegated access token. The RS can inspect both the user principal (sub) and the acting instance (act.sub) to make fine-grained access decisions. The DPoP proof proves the caller holds the instance's private key — matching the top-level cnf.jkt in the token.
annotations: §9.1: RS sees sub_profile='client_instance' in the act claim — applies instance-aware actor policy.
The delegation structure enables audit logging that captures both the authorizing user AND the specific instance that acted.
§8.6.2: The RS can enforce that the act.sub instance is a legitimate member of the client (verified by AS during issuance).
§12.6: The grant type (authorization_code) makes the delegation mode unambiguous — prevents mode-switch attacks.

steps.label: 200 OK + Resource
steps.explanation: The resource server returns the user's repositories. The response includes both the user's identity and the instance that acted on their behalf — enabling end-to-end accountability. The acting_instance field illustrates how the RS can surface instance identity for audit and compliance purposes.
annotations: The RS validated both the user principal (sub) and the acting instance (act.sub) before authorizing the request.
§12.9: Instance identifiers in access tokens should be treated as sensitive — equivalent to user tokens for logging purposes.
§10.1.2: The AS can revoke all tokens for a specific instance (act.sub) if the container is suspected compromised.
The full accountability chain: user authorized → AS validated instance → RS confirmed both → resource returned.
